title: NATO summit is Europe’s moment to turn crisis into opportunity
gist: European public confidence in Trump's US leadership has collapsed just as Washington cuts its military footprint in Europe and Russia builds toward 1.5 million troops focused on NATO — a combination Chatham House frames as a rare political opening for European governments to push through faster, more urgent defence modernization.

themes.theme: Ankara summit stakes: delivering, not promising

insights.claim: Where the 2025 Hague summit was about setting ambitious new defence spending targets, the 2026 Ankara summit will be about delivering on those promises, with an accounting of which allies have signed and paid for defence contracts versus those stuck at the rhetoric stage.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Marks a shift from pledge-tracking to execution-tracking at NATO summits, meaning contract awards and disbursed spending (not just stated targets) are now the relevant signal for defence-sector investors.
insights.locator: If last year’s summit was about setting ambitious new targets

insights.claim: Like the businessman he is, US President Donald Trump will want to see concrete numbers from NATO allies proving there is action behind the 2025 Hague summit's target of 5% of GDP spending on defence.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Trump's transactional framing means allied credibility at Ankara will be judged on hard spending data, raising pressure on laggard states.
insights.locator: Like the businessman he is, US President Donald Trump

themes.theme: Trust erosion as a strategic opening

insights.claim: Recent US announcements of reduced defence presence in Europe, combined with Trump's disparaging rhetoric on NATO and threats to take Greenland, have damaged political relations and eroded European public opinion on US leadership and reliability.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Deteriorating European public trust in the US is now a measurable political variable that governments can use to justify faster defence spending.
insights.locator: Recent announcements of reduced US defence presence

insights.claim: This erosion of trust in US leadership is not only a challenge but also an opportunity for European governments to build public understanding of and support for the funding and process changes needed to meet ambitious defence goals.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Reframes a negative geopolitical trend (declining US credibility) as political capital European leaders can spend on pushing through unpopular defence budget increases.
insights.locator: This erosion of trust undoubtedly poses a challenge but is also an opportunity

insights.claim: Pew Research Center polling across 10 countries finds a median of 81% of respondents lack confidence in Donald Trump doing the right thing regarding world affairs, sentiment Chatham House says can be used to motivate legislatures and reform bureaucracies toward national defence goals.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: An 81% negative-confidence reading is a concrete political mandate European leaders can cite when pushing defence budget increases through legislatures.
insights.locator: According to data from Pew Research Center

themes.theme: US drawdown and the capability gap

insights.claim: The US informed European officials in May 2026 that it would gradually withdraw military capabilities from NATO, including fighter jets, strategic bombers and warships, though the announcement remains short on details amid internal Trump administration disagreement over scale and timing.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Specific capability categories (fighter jets, bombers, warships) being withdrawn identify exactly where European allies need to backfill procurement.
insights.locator: The US informed European officials in May

insights.claim: NATO's top commander has reportedly said European allies have already filled most of the gaps left by US reductions and are exploring workarounds for the remaining shortfalls, though European allies do not currently have the scale of capabilities rumoured to be cut.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A gap between official reassurance ("most gaps filled") and the underlying capability shortfall is a risk to monitor going into Ankara's announcements.
insights.locator: According to a recent report, NATO’s top commander

insights.claim: Experts estimate it could take up to 25 years to fill the capability gap if the US were to leave NATO altogether, a fear that remains live despite current reassurances.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A 25-year rebuild timeline underscores the scale of European dependency on US capability and the tail risk if the relationship deteriorates further.
insights.locator: Should this happen, experts estimate it could take up to 25 years

insights.claim: Unlike NATO, Russia does not face domestic pushback on military expenditures, which strengthens its ability to sustain a prolonged war-economy build-up.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Asymmetric domestic political constraints (contested defence budgets in Europe vs. none in Russia) structurally favor Russia's ability to sustain military spending over time.
insights.locator: This is even more important as Russia does not face domestic push back

insights.claim: Although Russia's war campaign against Ukraine has failed to achieve its military goals, Russia is now rooted in a war economy, fielding war-tested troops and preparing for a prolonged period of conflict, with the Kremlin building toward 1.5 million military service personnel and 17 new manoeuvre divisions primarily focused on conflict with NATO.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Concrete force-size targets (1.5 million personnel, 17 divisions) give NATO planners a quantifiable adversary benchmark for capability planning.
insights.locator: The Kremlin is taking steps towards building a military

insights.claim: Recent Western discussions suggesting pressure on Russia's economy from Ukrainian drone strikes and sanctions is reaching an inflection point are 'perhaps comforting but miss the point', because for Russia, NATO's Nordic expansion and full support for Ukraine remain 'evergreen existential threats' regardless of economic pressure.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Pushes back against the idea that economic pressure alone will change Russian behavior — implies sanctions/drone strikes shouldn't be relied on as a substitute for NATO capability build-up.
insights.locator: Recent discussions in Western capitals that the pressure

insights.claim: The Kremlin now perceives that the US is distracted in other parts of the world and deprioritizing Europe, which shapes Russia's strategic calculus.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Russian perception of US disengagement from Europe is itself a driver of Russian risk tolerance, independent of actual US capability levels.
insights.locator: And the Kremlin now sees that the US is distracted

insights.claim: European allies should keep the United States engaged while building a modern European security architecture that better meets today's threats and is less dependent on American assets.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Sets the strategic goal against which Ankara summit outcomes on European capability-building should be measured.
insights.locator: In Ankara, European allies should align to keep

insights.claim: The first recommended action is getting the public narrative right: European publics need a unified understanding of Putin's mindset and Trump's withdrawal, with the message that panic is not the answer but revitalized budgets and coordinated, timely defence investment are.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Effective public communication is framed as a strategic instrument on par with budget decisions, not a secondary PR concern.
insights.locator: First, get the public narrative right

insights.claim: The second recommended action is to look to the Nordic defence industry model, which centers consistent cooperation with industry from defining needs through procurement, and to US moves to use AI in procurement processes to streamline production and shorten delivery timelines for defence capabilities.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Identifies the Nordic industry-cooperation model and AI-assisted procurement as concrete, exportable templates for speeding up European defence acquisition.
insights.locator: Second, look to the Nordic defence industry model
